presentation.title: AI Workbench Local — Policy / Contract Comparison
presentation.subtitle: Executive comparison review of grounded document differences
presentation.author: AI Workbench Local

executive_summary:
**Executive Comparison:**  
Meridian Holdings' Information Security Policy evolved from v3.1 to v3.2 with significant enhancements in incident response, access controls, and log retention. While v3.1 established foundational controls, it left critical customer-data incident notification timing undefined, creating compliance and response risks. v3.2 addresses this gap by introducing specific, time-bound requirements—including 24-hour escalation for customer data incidents, universal MFA, and quarterly privileged access reviews—reflecting a shift toward proactive, audit-ready governance aligned with regulatory expectations.

- v3.1 lacked a defined timeframe for notifying stakeholders about critical customer-data incidents; v3.2 mandates 24-hour escalation for such events.  
- v3.1 required MFA and ticket-based access but did not enforce MFA universally; v3.2 extends mandatory MFA to all workforce accounts.  
- v3.1 did not specify frequency for privileged access reviews; v3.2 introduces quarterly reviews to tighten access governance.  
- v3.1 referenced log retention and vendor evidence requirements without specifying duration; v3.2 mandates 12-month tamper-evident log retention.  
- v3.1 noted ongoing governance maturity through annual updates; v3.2 demonstrates proactive risk mitigation in response to evolving threats and regulations.  
- The absence of explicit enforcement mechanisms in v3.1 persists as a limitation, though v3.2 strengthens accountability through defined timelines and controls.  

**Recommendation:** Adopt and enforce v3.2 immediately to close critical response gaps, improve regulatory compliance, and strengthen defense against identity-based and supply chain threats. Prioritize training on the 24-hour incident escalation process and quarterly access reviews to ensure operational effectiveness. Monitor enforcement mechanisms in future updates to close remaining governance gaps.

While v3.1 established foundational controls, it left critical customer-data incident notification timing undefined, creating compliance and response risks. v3.2 addresses this gap by introducing specific, time-bound requirements—including 24-hour escalation for customer data incidents, universal MFA, and quarterly privileged access reviews—reflecting a shift toward proactive, audit-ready governance aligned with regulatory expectations.

v3.1 did not specify frequency for privileged access reviews; v3.2 introduces quarterly reviews to tighten access governance.
